insights: Breadth: 14/16 stocks up — broadly bullish today
context: Broad buying today — most large-caps rose. Broad-based rallies are generally healthier than a narrow one led by 1–2 heavyweights.
Money rotated into IT and out of Pharma. Sector leadership shifts often — chasing last month's hottest sector is a classic beginner trap.
India VIX is low (13.15) — calm, low expected volatility. Calm markets breed complacency; a good time to check your asset allocation, not to chase risk.

gk.t: A teaspoon of a neutron star would weigh billions of tonnes
gk.teaser: The densest matter known to science.
gk.more: When a massive star collapses, its core can be crushed into a neutron star — an object the size of a city but heavier than the Sun. Matter is packed so tightly that a sugar-cube-sized piece would weigh billions of tonnes on Earth. A single teaspoon would outweigh Mount Everest many times over.

gk.t: An octopus has three hearts and blue blood
gk.teaser: Two hearts feed the gills, one feeds the body.
gk.more: Two of an octopus's hearts pump blood through the gills, while the third circulates it to the rest of the body. Its blood is blue because it uses copper-based hemocyanin, rather than iron-based hemoglobin, to carry oxygen — which works better in cold, low-oxygen water. The main heart even stops beating when the octopus swims, which is why they often prefer to crawl.

gk.t: The world's first stock exchange opened in Amsterdam
gk.teaser: The Dutch East India Company sold the first public shares.
gk.more: In 1602 the Dutch East India Company (VOC) became the first firm to issue tradable shares to the public, and the Amsterdam exchange grew up around trading them. It pioneered ideas we still use daily — shares, dividends and speculation. It also witnessed some of the earliest recorded short selling and market bubbles.

gk.t: History's worst inflation doubled prices every 15 hours
gk.more: In mid-1946 Hungary's pengő suffered the most extreme hyperinflation ever recorded, with prices roughly doubling every 15 hours. The state issued a banknote worth 100 quintillion pengő, the highest denomination ever put into circulation. The currency was scrapped and replaced by the forint, which Hungary still uses today.

gk.teaser: When its west wakes, its far east is already asleep.
gk.more: Russia is so wide that it covers 11 time zones — more than any other single continuous stretch of land. When it is morning in Kaliningrad in the west, it is already evening in Kamchatka on the Pacific coast. France, counting its scattered overseas territories, technically touches even more zones, but Russia's are all contiguous.

gk.t: The Dead Sea is Earth's lowest exposed land
gk.teaser: Its shore sits over 400 metres below sea level.
gk.more: The surface of the Dead Sea lies more than 430 metres below sea level, the lowest dry land on the planet, and it keeps dropping roughly a metre a year. It is nearly ten times saltier than the ocean — so dense that swimmers float effortlessly. Almost nothing lives in it, which is how it earned its name.

gk.teaser: The Indian plate keeps pushing it higher.
gk.more: The Himalayas rose when the Indian tectonic plate collided with Asia, and that collision is still underway. Everest gains a few millimetres each year as the plates keep pressing together. Earthquakes can nudge its height up or down, one reason it has been remeasured over time — now officially about 8,849 metres.

gk.t: The first computer 'bug' was a real insect
gk.teaser: A moth got trapped in a 1947 machine.
gk.more: In 1947, operators of the Harvard Mark II computer found a moth stuck in a relay, causing a malfunction. They taped it into the logbook with the note 'first actual case of bug being found.' The pioneering programmer Grace Hopper helped popularise the tale, cementing 'debugging' in the language — though engineers had loosely used 'bug' for faults even earlier.

gk.t: Computing power has doubled roughly every two years
gk.teaser: Moore's Law shaped the entire digital age.
gk.more: In 1965 Intel co-founder Gordon Moore observed that the number of transistors on a chip was doubling about every year; he revised the pace to roughly every two years in 1975, and the industry turned that observation into a roadmap. This relentless doubling is why today's smartphone vastly outmuscles the computers that guided Apollo 11 to the Moon. The pace is finally slowing as transistors shrink toward the size of a few atoms.

In October 1969, researchers tried to send the word 'LOGIN' between two computers on ARPANET, the internet's ancestor. The network crashed after just two letters, so the first message ever transmitted was 'lo.' They fixed it within the hour, and the full link soon worked — the humble start of a technology that now connects billions.

gkMore.teaser: Land heats faster than sea — wind follows.
gkMore.more: By June the subcontinent's landmass is much hotter than the Indian Ocean, creating a low-pressure zone that pulls moisture-laden winds northeast. They break on the Western Ghats and Himalayas as rain. About 70% of India's annual rainfall arrives in these four months, which is why a 'bad monsoon' moves inflation, farm incomes and even elections.

gkMore.t: Ashoka: the emperor who renounced war
gkMore.teaser: His wheel sits at the centre of the tricolour.
gkMore.more: After the bloody Kalinga war (~261 BCE), Mauryan emperor Ashoka embraced Buddhism and spread edicts on rock pillars across the subcontinent. The Ashoka Chakra on India's flag and the Lion Capital national emblem both come from his pillars at Sarnath.

WWI started with an assassination in Sarajevo and ended empires (Ottoman, Austro-Hungarian, Russian Tsar). WWII began with Germany invading Poland and ended with atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki. Over 2.5 million Indian soldiers served in WWII — the largest volunteer army in history.

gkMore.teaser: Founded 1945 by 51 countries so WWIII never happens.
gkMore.more: Headquartered in New York, its Security Council has five veto-holding permanent members: US, UK, France, Russia, China. India is a founding member and the largest cumulative contributor of UN peacekeeping troops, but its long push for a permanent Security Council seat remains unresolved.

gkMore.teaser: One night in November ended Europe's clearest Cold War symbol.
gkMore.more: Built in 1961 to stop East Germans fleeing to the capitalist West, the wall cut Berlin in two for 28 years. Its fall on 9 November 1989 triggered German reunification within a year and previewed the Soviet collapse of 1991.

gkMore.teaser: A military pact where attacking one member means attacking all.
gkMore.more: Founded 1949, the North Atlantic Treaty Organization's Article 5 collective-defence clause has been invoked once — after 9/11. It expanded eastward after the Cold War, which Russia cites among grievances behind the Ukraine conflict. India is not a member and stays formally non-aligned.

gkMore.teaser: 27 countries, one market, 21 sharing the euro.
gkMore.more: Born from post-WWII efforts to bind France and Germany economically so they'd never fight again, the European Union lets goods, services, money and people move freely across members. Brexit (2020) made the UK the only country ever to leave.

gkMore.t: Why there's an International Date Line
gkMore.teaser: Cross it westward and you skip a day.
gkMore.more: The Earth has 24 time zones; somewhere the calendar must flip. The line zigzags through the Pacific to avoid splitting countries. Fly Delhi to San Francisco and you can land at an earlier local time than you took off — the date line gives the day back.

gkMore.teaser: It replaced a maze of central and state levies.
gkMore.more: The Goods and Services Tax merged excise, VAT, service tax and more into one destination-based tax with slabs (0/5/12/18/28%). It made interstate commerce dramatically simpler — trucks no longer queue at state borders for tax checks — and every invoice feeds a single national IT backbone.

gkMore.teaser: In 2023 India became the first to land near the lunar south pole.
gkMore.more: After Chandrayaan-2's lander crashed in 2019, ISRO's Vikram lander touched down softly on 23 August 2023 — making India the fourth country to soft-land on the Moon and the first at the south polar region, where ice deposits could support future missions. Mission cost: roughly ₹615 crore, less than many Hollywood films.

learn.title: Market cap, and why price ≠ size
learn.teaser: A ₹5,000 stock can be a smaller company than a ₹500 one.
body: Market capitalisation = share price × number of shares. MRF's famous ₹1+ lakh share price makes it nowhere near India's biggest company; Reliance's much cheaper shares multiply across far more of them.

Caps define categories — large (top 100), mid (101–250), small (rest) by SEBI's rules — which matters because mutual funds are mandated to buy within their category. A stock crossing a cap boundary mechanically gains or loses buyers.

learn.title: Percentiles: how analysts avoid lying
learn.teaser: p50, p90, p99 — the shape of reality, not one number.
body: An average API response of 200ms hides that 1% of users wait 4 seconds. p50 (median) shows the typical case, p90 the bad day, p99 the disaster tail. Same for delivery times, load times, ticket resolution.
Reporting reflex: any latency, wait-time or money metric deserves at least p50 + p90. If someone gives you only the mean, they're either new or hiding the tail.

learn.title: The 1962 war India doesn't celebrate
learn.teaser: A month-long border war with China reshaped Indian defence policy.
body: In October 1962 Chinese forces advanced across the disputed Himalayan border (Aksai Chin, NEFA — today's Arunachal), overwhelming underprepared Indian positions before declaring a unilateral ceasefire. Nehru's reputation never fully recovered.
The war triggered India's military modernisation and its tilt toward Soviet arms, and the unresolved Line of Actual Control remains the flashpoint behind standoffs like Galwan 2020.

learn.title: What inflation really is — and why a little is healthy
learn.teaser: Rising prices aren't always bad news.
body: Inflation is the general rise in the prices of goods and services over time, which quietly erodes the purchasing power of money — the same 100 rupees buys less next year than it does today. It is measured using price indices such as the Consumer Price Index (CPI), which tracks a representative basket of household spending. In India, the Reserve Bank of India is legally tasked with keeping CPI inflation around 4%, within a tolerance band of 2% to 6%.

Inflation has two broad drivers. 'Demand-pull' inflation happens when too much money chases too few goods, often after rapid growth or heavy money-printing. 'Cost-push' inflation comes from rising input costs, such as a spike in crude oil that raises transport and manufacturing prices across the board. A mild, steady rate of inflation is actually considered healthy: it nudges people to spend and invest rather than hoard cash.

The real danger lies at the extremes. Deflation — falling prices — can be worse than mild inflation, because people delay purchases expecting things to get cheaper, stalling the economy, while debts become heavier in real terms. At the other end, hyperinflation can destroy a currency entirely. This is why central banks watch inflation obsessively and adjust interest rates to keep it in a narrow, predictable range.

learn.title: What actually happens when you buy a share
learn.teaser: You're buying a small slice of a real company.
body: A share is a unit of ownership in a company. When a business wants to raise money to grow, it can sell ownership stakes to the public through an Initial Public Offering (IPO). After that, those shares trade between investors on stock exchanges such as the BSE and NSE in India. The price of a share at any moment reflects the collective judgement of buyers and sellers about the company's future profits.

Because tracking thousands of individual companies is hard, markets use indices — the Sensex and Nifty in India, or the S&P 500 in the US — which bundle major stocks into a single number that summarises overall market mood. Prices move constantly on earnings reports, economic news, interest-rate changes and plain sentiment. Over the long run, stock markets have historically outpaced inflation, but they can be brutally volatile over months or even years.

At its best, the stock market is a machine for channelling ordinary people's savings into productive enterprise, and for letting them share in the growth of the economy. But shares carry real risk — companies can fail, and prices can fall sharply. This is why long horizons, diversification, and low-cost index funds are the standard advice for most individual investors rather than betting on single stocks.

learn.title: GDP: the number that rules economics — and its blind spots
body: Gross Domestic Product (GDP) is the total market value of all goods and services produced within a country over a given period, usually a year or a quarter. It is the headline measure of an economy's size, and its growth rate is watched closely by governments, investors and voters. India is among the world's largest economies by total GDP, though its GDP per person — the total divided by population — remains far lower than that of rich nations.

The trouble is that GDP was never designed to measure how well people are actually living. It counts activity regardless of whether it improves lives: cleaning up an oil spill or rebuilding after a disaster adds to GDP, while unpaid housework and caregiving, which have enormous real value, are left out entirely. It also says nothing about how income is distributed, so a country can post strong growth while most of the gains flow to a small elite.

Because of these blind spots, economists supplement GDP with other measures. The Human Development Index blends income with health and education; other indicators try to account for inequality or environmental damage. GDP remains indispensable as a quick gauge of economic size and momentum, but a curious observer should treat it as one dashboard dial among many, not the whole picture.

learn.title: How central banks steer an economy with one main lever
learn.teaser: The interest rate is the economy's thermostat.
body: A central bank — the Reserve Bank of India at home, the Federal Reserve in the US — is the institution that manages a nation's money. Its most powerful tool is the benchmark interest rate, in India called the repo rate, which is the rate at which it lends to commercial banks. Changing this rate ripples out to the interest on home loans, business credit and savings across the whole economy.

The logic is like a thermostat. When inflation runs too hot, the central bank raises rates, making borrowing costlier, cooling spending and investment, and easing price pressure. When the economy is sluggish, it cuts rates to make credit cheap and encourage activity. Central banks also manage the money supply, hold the country's foreign-exchange reserves, oversee the banking system, and act as a 'lender of last resort' during financial panics.

This power comes with hard trade-offs. Fighting inflation with high rates can slow growth and raise unemployment, so central bankers are constantly balancing competing risks. That is also why their independence from short-term political pressure is prized: governments facing elections are tempted to keep money cheap, but a credible, independent central bank can make the unpopular choices needed to keep prices stable over the long run.

learn.title: Why starting to invest early beats investing a lot
learn.teaser: Time is the most powerful force in finance.
body: Compound interest is often called the eighth wonder of the world, and for good reason. When you invest, you earn returns not just on your original money but also on all the returns it has already generated. Over short periods this looks unremarkable, but over decades it snowballs dramatically — the growth curve bends sharply upward as the gains start generating their own gains.

This is why time matters more than the size of your contributions. Someone who invests a modest amount every month starting in their twenties can end up with far more than someone who invests much larger sums but only begins in their forties, simply because the early money had more years to compound. In India, Systematic Investment Plans (SIPs) into equity mutual funds are a popular way to harness this, though what really matters is investing regularly and staying invested through the ups and downs.

The same force works in reverse and can quietly ruin people. High-interest debt — credit-card balances, some personal loans — compounds against you just as relentlessly, so an unpaid balance can balloon. The practical takeaways are simple but powerful: start early, keep costs and needless debt low, focus on real returns after inflation, and let time do the heavy lifting.
